questions.section: 19.1 All the Places Patterns Can Be Used
questions.prompt: Which of the following are places where patterns can be used in Rust? Select all that apply.

options.C: Function parameters, e.g. `fn print_coordinates(&(x, y): &(i32, i32))`
options.D: The loop variable of a `for` loop, e.g. `for (index, value) in v.iter().enumerate()`
options.E: The operands of arithmetic operators like `+`

questions.explanation: Patterns appear in `match` arms, `if let` and `while let` expressions, `for` loop bindings, `let` statements, and function or closure parameters (which can destructure tuples directly). Operands of arithmetic operators are ordinary expressions, not pattern positions.

questions.section: 19.1 All the Places Patterns Can Be Used
questions.prompt: Consider the statement `let x = 5;`. Which statement about `let` and patterns is true?

options.A: `let` statements do not involve patterns; only `match` expressions use patterns
options.B: Every `let` statement has the form `let PATTERN = EXPRESSION;` — in `let x = 5;` the name `x` is itself an irrefutable pattern that binds the whole value
options.C: Patterns in `let` only work with tuples, like `let (a, b) = (1, 2);`
options.D: `let x = 5;` uses a pattern only if you add an explicit type annotation

questions.explanation: A plain variable name is a pattern that matches anything and binds the entire value to that name. That is why destructuring like `let (x, y, z) = (1, 2, 3);` works: it is the same `let PATTERN = EXPRESSION;` form. Function parameters are patterns in exactly the same way.

questions.section: 19.1 All the Places Patterns Can Be Used
questions.explanation: The chain mixes `if let`, `else if`, and `else if let` with plain boolean conditions. `favorite_color` is `None` (first branch skipped), `is_tuesday` is false (second skipped), and `"34".parse()` yields `Ok(34)`, so the shadowed `age` is `34`, which is greater than 30 — printing `Using purple`. Note the compiler does not check `if let` chains for exhaustiveness.

questions.section: 19.1 All the Places Patterns Can Be Used

options.C: It loops forever because the pattern always matches
options.D: Compile error: `while let` cannot use the refutable pattern `Some(top)`

questions.explanation: `while let` runs as long as the pattern keeps matching. `pop` removes elements from the end of the vector, returning `Some(3)`, `Some(2)`, `Some(1)`, and finally `None`, which fails to match `Some(top)` and ends the loop. Refutable patterns are exactly what `while let` is for.

questions.section: 19.1 All the Places Patterns Can Be Used
questions.prompt: What is the first line this program prints?

options.D: Compile error: a `for` loop variable cannot destructure a tuple

questions.explanation: The position after `for` is a pattern. `enumerate` produces tuples `(index, item)`, so the pattern `(index, value)` destructures each tuple: the first item is `(0, 'a')`, printing `a is at index 0`.

questions.section: 19.2 Refutability: Whether a Pattern Might Fail to Match
questions.prompt: Patterns are either refutable (can fail to match, like `Some(x)`) or irrefutable (match anything, like `x`). Which constructs accept only irrefutable patterns?
questions.explanation: `let` statements, function parameters, and `for` loops can't do anything meaningful when a pattern fails to match, so they require irrefutable patterns. `if let`, `while let`, and `let...else` are designed to handle possible match failure, so they accept refutable patterns.

questions.section: 19.2 Refutability: Whether a Pattern Might Fail to Match
questions.prompt: This program does not compile. What is the bug?

options.A: `Some(x)` needs an explicit type annotation to compile
options.B: `let` requires an irrefutable pattern, but `Some(x)` is refutable — it doesn't cover `None` (error E0005); use `let...else` or `if let` instead
options.C: You cannot destructure an `Option` with a pattern; you must call `.unwrap()`
options.D: It compiles fine but panics at runtime because the value is `None`

questions.explanation: The compiler rejects this with `error[E0005]: refutable pattern in local binding` because `let` has nowhere to go if the value is `None`. Fix it with `let Some(x) = some_option_value else { return; };` or `if let Some(x) = some_option_value { ... }`, both of which handle the non-matching case.

questions.section: 19.2 Refutability: Whether a Pattern Might Fail to Match
questions.prompt: What happens when you compile and run this program?

options.A: Compile error: `if let` requires a refutable pattern
options.B: It compiles and prints `5`, but the compiler emits a warning that the `if let` pattern is irrefutable
options.C: It compiles and runs silently with no warning

questions.explanation: Giving `if let` (or `let...else`) a pattern that always matches, like a plain variable name `x`, is accepted but pointless — the conditional adds nothing — so the compiler warns about an irrefutable pattern. The block still runs and prints `5`.

options.A: `Matched, y = 10` then `at the end: x = Some(5), y = 10`
options.B: `Matched, y = 5` then `at the end: x = Some(5), y = 10`
options.C: `Matched, y = 5` then `at the end: x = Some(5), y = 5`
options.D: `Default case, x = Some(5)` then `at the end: x = Some(5), y = 10`

questions.explanation: Named variables in patterns are new bindings. `Some(y)` in the second arm does NOT compare against the outer `y = 10`; it introduces a fresh `y` that shadows it and binds whatever is inside the `Some` — here `5`. The shadow ends with the arm, so the final line still sees the outer `y = 10`.

questions.prompt: An outer variable `y` already exists, and a `match` arm uses the pattern `Some(y)`. Which statements are true? Select all that apply.

options.A: The pattern introduces a brand-new variable `y` that shadows the outer `y` inside the arm
options.B: The new `y` binds the value found inside the `Some`, regardless of the outer `y`'s value
options.C: To test the inner value against the outer `y`, you can use a match guard such as `Some(n) if n == y`
options.D: The shadowing ends when the match arm's scope ends; the outer `y` is unchanged afterwards
options.E: `Some(y)` only matches when the value equals `Some(10)` if the outer `y` is `10`

questions.explanation: Inside patterns, a bare name always creates a new binding — it never compares against an existing variable, so E is the classic misconception. Because a guard's `if` expression is not a pattern, it can refer to the outer `y`, which is how you compare against it: `Some(n) if n == y`.

options.D: Compile error: ranges are not allowed in patterns

questions.explanation: `|` is the or operator in patterns (`1 | 2` matches either literal), and `3..=5` is an inclusive range pattern that matches 3, 4, or 5. Range patterns also work with `char`, e.g. `'a'..='j'`. Since `x` is 5, the second arm matches.

options.D: Compile error: a struct pattern cannot mix shorthand fields and literal values

questions.explanation: Struct patterns can mix shorthand bindings (`x` creates a variable from field `x`) with literal tests (`y: 0` requires the field to equal 0). The first arm needs `y == 0`, which fails since `y` is 7. The second arm needs `x == 0`, which succeeds and binds `y = 7`. Arms are tried in order.

questions.prompt: What is wrong with this code?

options.A: Nothing — it compiles and prints `Some numbers: 4`
options.B: Compile error: `..` can only be used once per tuple pattern — using it on both sides of `second` is ambiguous about how many values to skip
options.C: Compile error: tuples cannot be matched with `..` at all
options.D: It compiles and prints `Some numbers: 8` because `second` grabs the middle element

questions.explanation: `..` ignores 'the rest' of the values, but Rust must be able to tell unambiguously which values it covers. `(first, .., last)` is fine because `..` appears once, but `(.., second, ..)` is rejected with an error that `..` can only be used once per tuple pattern.

questions.prompt: This program fails to compile. Why?

options.A: It actually compiles and prints `found a string` then `Some("Hello!")`
options.B: Unlike `_`, the name `_s` still binds the value — it moves the `String` out of `s`, so the later `println!("{s:?}")` uses a moved value; write `Some(_)` (which never binds) or match on `&s`
options.C: `_s` is not a valid identifier because it starts with an underscore
options.D: It compiles but panics at runtime when printing `s`

questions.explanation: An underscore-prefixed name like `_s` only silences the unused-variable warning; it is still a real binding, so ownership of the `String` moves into it and `s` becomes unusable. The bare wildcard `_` never binds at all, so `if let Some(_) = s` leaves `s` intact and the program compiles.

options.C: Compile error: match guards cannot be combined with `|` patterns

questions.explanation: A match guard applies to the entire `|` chain: the arm behaves like `(4 | 5 | 6) if y`, not `4 | 5 | (6 if y)`. Even though `x` is 4 and matches the pattern, the guard `if y` is false, so the arm is rejected and the `_` arm prints `no`.

questions.prompt: Match guards (an extra `if` condition after a pattern, like `Some(x) if x % 2 == 0`) add expressive power. What is their downside?

options.A: A guard makes the arm's pattern irrefutable
options.B: The compiler does not take guard conditions into account when checking exhaustiveness, so a `match` relying on guards still needs arms that cover all cases without them
options.C: Guards may only refer to variables bound inside the pattern, never to outer variables
options.D: The guard runs after the arm body has already executed

questions.explanation: Guards can express conditions patterns alone cannot (including comparing against outer variables, which sidesteps the shadowing problem), but the compiler cannot reason about arbitrary `if` expressions, so guarded arms don't count toward exhaustiveness checking. The guard is evaluated after the pattern matches and before the body runs.

options.B: Found an id in another range
options.D: Compile error: a binding cannot be combined with a range pattern

questions.explanation: The `@` operator both tests a value against a pattern and binds it: `id_variable @ 3..=7` matches because 5 is in the range, and captures 5 in `id_variable`. By contrast, the second arm's plain `10..=12` tests the field but binds nothing, so its body could not use the id value.

questions.prompt: Which statements about destructuring patterns are true? Select all that apply.

options.A: The struct field shorthand `Point { x, y }` creates variables named `x` and `y` from the corresponding fields
options.B: Enum variants are destructured with patterns that mirror the variant's shape, e.g. `Message::ChangeColor(r, g, b)` for a tuple-like variant and `Message::Move { x, y }` for a struct-like one
options.C: Patterns can be nested, e.g. `Message::ChangeColor(Color::Hsv(h, s, v))` matches one enum stored inside another enum's variant
options.D: `Point { x, .. }` binds `x` and ignores all remaining fields of the struct
options.E: Destructuring works for structs and enums but not for tuples nested inside them

questions.explanation: Patterns compose: structs, enums, tuples, and references can all be destructured and nested arbitrarily, such as `let ((feet, inches), Point { x, y }) = ...`, so E is false. `..` inside a struct pattern skips whatever fields are not named, and the shorthand avoids writing `x: x`.
